title: [CI:DOCS] GHA: Support testing build/sign workflows
body:
Neither `release` nor `workflow_dispatch` triggers may be tested inside a PR context.  The workflow steps always run from what's already committed to `main`.  Rather than waiting for a release to discover some unforeseen workflow problem, allow manual runs to optionally skip the release upload step (by default).

TODO:
- [ ] Test these changes on a throwaway `main` branch somewhere.
- [x] See if it's worth calling to a child-workflow to reduce steps duplication.

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
None
```

comments.body: Oh I gotcha, yeah, good idea, thanks!
/hold needs_testing

LGTM

@cevich the powershell changes looks correct to me, i don't see any issue, but would recommend doing a test release in case I missed anything.

Thanks for taking a look @n1hility 

> but would recommend doing a test release in case I missed anything.

Ugg, yes, I'm dreading it, but do intend to test.  GHA makes this important step SOOOOOO painful, esp. when secrets are involved :cry:

I think I may try mocking out all the building and signing parts first, to check the dry-run logic is correct.

@n1hility and @ashley-cui are there commands I could add to the workflow that would verify the binary is correctly signed w/o attempting to install it?

> Thanks for taking a look @n1hility
> 
> > but would recommend doing a test release in case I missed anything.
> 
> Ugg, yes, I'm dreading it, but do intend to test. GHA makes this important step SOOOOOO painful, esp. when secrets are involved 😢

Ah yeah, not fun. The way I have gone about this is to create a cloned repo as a new gh repo and register the secrets there as well, then you can just happily create and delete a bunch of bogus releases.

Another option could be to do a pre-release of a bogus version tag on the official repo, and then just never publish it. It should run then as well.

> 
> I think I may try mocking out all the building and signing parts first, to check the dry-run logic is correct.
> 
> @n1hility and @ashley-cui are there commands I could add to the workflow that would verify the binary is correctly signed w/o attempting to install it?

Yes IIRC the GHA runners all have vscode installed, so signtool.exe can be used which is located at: `C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Kits\10\bin\x64\signtool.exe`

if you do `signtool.exe verify /pa podman*-setup.exe` that should do it (note you need the /pa otherwise it uses the driver CA which will fail). Note that all the executables installed and used by the installer are recursively signed, but the signature verification doesn't know the format, so will only confirm the authenticity of the outter executable. If you were looking to validate everything you would need to unpack it into its constituent parts and check each one. For that you can use the Wix toolset dark.exe command (also installed on GHA IIRC) to unbundle the exe, and then unbundle the elements of the nested msi.

Thanks Jason, yeah it's the 'copy the secrets' part that's an extra PITA b/c there are so damn many of them (for both Mac and Win).  I think I'll just mock-out the actual build/signing stuff for testing.

Thanks for the test command, once this dry-run thing is in I can look into adding that and the equivalent for mac.

Tests:
* Manual dry-run w/ non-existent version
  * [Windows](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742533547) - behaved as expected, though there is a minor bug in the workflow dispatch dialog text: `Release version to build and upload (e.g. "4.2.1")` -> `...upload (e.g. "v4.2.1")` (the `v` is standard for podman releases).
  * [Mac](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742530188) - behaved as expected, workflow dispatch dialog text mentions the `v`.
* After pushing mock `v0.0.1` tag
  * Windows - workflow did not execute (expected)
  * Mac - workflow did not execute (expected)
* After creating a `v0.0.1` release w/o uploading anything
  * Windows
    * [Release Created](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742626141) - behaved as designed: workflow did not build or sign b/c release asset `podman-remote-release-windows_amd64.zip` does not exist.  Confirmed that Podman `RELEASE_PROCESS.md` indicates this file must be uploaded manually when creating a new release.
    * [Release Published](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742626131) - behaved exactly the same as `Release Created` run (above)
  * Mac
    * [Release Created](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742626139) - Behaved as designed/mocked.  Built `podman-installer-macos-amd64.pkg`, `podman-installer-macos-arm64.pkg`, and shasums files.  Saved the two `.pkg` files as workflow artifacts.  Uploaded all three files to the `v0.0.1` release page.
    * [Release Published](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742626132) - Behaved unexpectedly.  May have been a TOCTOU race, since workflow ran about the same time as `Release Created`.  It Built all three files just fine, but uploading them to the release page failed with an "already exists" type error.  I would consider this a bug since the Windows workflow accounts for this possibility - though it could easily be "fixed" by printing a warning and ignoring the error (i.e. NOT uploading the built files).  Though ideally it would do a simple curl check on the assets, similar to how the Windows workflow operates (see `check.ps1` script).
*  Manual dry-run w/ existing `v0.0.1` tag and release
   * [Windows w/o uploading the zip](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742974935) - Fails as expected due to missing release asset zip file.  There is a bug here though, in that it's not obvious this was the problem.  This could be fixed by validating `steps.check.outputs.upload_asset_name` is non-empty, and printing a helpful error if so.
   * [Windows after uploading `podman-remote-release-windows_amd64.zip` file to the release page](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5743051577) - Failed as above, but likely due to the 'Check' step being incorrectly skipped.  This step should run even in dry-run mode.  I'll fix it in this PR.
   * [Mac](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5742913466) - behaved as expected.  Built all three files and saved them into an archive.  Did **NOT** attempt to upload them to the release page.
* Added a commit to always perform the windows `Check` step, and verify the required uploaded file was found - printing a helpful error if not.  Tagged and created a release `v0.0.2`:
  * [Mac Release Published](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5743244252) - Behaved as designed.  Built and uploaded all three release asset files.
  * Mac Release Created - workflow was not triggered.  I don't fully understand why.
  * [Windows Release Published](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5743448502) - Behaved as designed, new check worked well.  Uploaded `podman-0.0.2-setup.exe` release asset.
  * Windows Release Created - also did not run.  Ignoring.
* Manual dry-run on existing `0.0.2` mock release
  * [Mac](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5743594783) - Behaves as designed.  Builds and creates workflow artifact of all three files.
  * [Windows](https://github.com/containers/automation_sandbox/actions/runs/5743550734) Fails unexpectedly - When .exe release asset already exists, Check workflow step does not set `UPLOAD_ASSET_NAME` (due to `if ($code -eq 2)` condition).  I think this is a bug, and maybe a sign the Windows workflow is over-complicated.  I think having it behave something like the mac workflow would be simpler and improved.

For Macs: 

You can check if the .pkg is notarized by using 
```
spctl -a -vvv -t install contrib/pkginstaller/out/podman-installer-macos-arm64.pkg
spctl -a -vvv -t install contrib/pkginstaller/out/podman-installer-macos-amd64.pkg
```

To check if the binaries are correctly signed, you can use 
`codesign -dv --verbose=4 contrib/pkginstaller/out/packaging/root/podman/bin/podman`
Thanks @ashley-cui I'll look into adding a check for that once this PR is done.  It will be a good excuse to try out the dry-run feature.

Testing conclusion:

So in all cases, with a minor tweak and a check the dry-run changes operate properly.  However, IMHO there are a number of followup items that can go into separate PRs (I'm open for debate on all):

1. Add mandatory post-build/sign tests (commands in comments above) to verify the signed things are actually signed.  If not, still upload the workflow artifacts (to aid in debugging) but then fail the workflow with a helpful `::error::` message.
2. For the windows workflow.  It should check for the release asset `podman-remote-release-windows_amd64.zip`.  If it doesn't exist, fail the workflow with a helpful `::error::You didn't follow the podman RELEASE_PROCESS.md doc precisely enough.` (joking example) message.
3. For both mac and windows, for actual runs (not dry-runs), I think we can simplify/improve the workflows by simply always doing a `gh release upload --clobber $version <files>` at the end.  Reason being, we have the safety-net of a check (see item 1).  Alternatively, we could have a 'overwrite' input variable to control the behavior instead.  In the *unlikely* event of an accidental overwrite, there's a 90-day recovery window before prior workflow artifacts are deleted.

@ashley-cui @n1hility PTAL when you have a chance, I think this is ready.  Though we should probably coordinate the followup work.  I'm a bit nervous to try and touch the windows/powershell scripts myself.

> 1. Add mandatory post-build/sign tests (commands in comments above) to verify the signed things are actually signed.  If not, still upload the workflow artifacts (to aid in debugging) but then fail the workflow with a helpful `::error::` message.

SGTM

> 3. For both mac and windows, for actual runs (not dry-runs), I think we can simplify/improve the workflows by simply always doing a `gh release upload --clobber $version <files>` at the end.  Reason being, we have the safety-net of a check (see item 1).  Alternatively, we could have a 'overwrite' input variable to control the behavior instead.  In the _unlikely_ event of an accidental overwrite, there's a 90-day recovery window before prior workflow artifacts are deleted.

The only possible mishap here is that we end up with a shasums file that includes old shasums, since we're not checking the contents of the shasums file, just appending to it. The mac script uploads the pkgs as an Actions artifact regardless of whether the GH upload succeeds or not. I'd rather err on the side of safety and not clobber, but I also don't feel super strongly about this.

/hold cancel

> file that includes old shasums

It's easy to include that file into the workflow artifacts.
Whew! Okay, I think I have a bunch of followup-issues to open...

> 3. For both mac and windows, for actual runs (not dry-runs), I think we can simplify/improve the workflows by simply always doing a `gh release upload --clobber $version <files>` at the end.  Reason being, we have the safety-net of a check (see item 1).  Alternatively, we could have a 'overwrite' input variable to control the behavior instead.  In the _unlikely_ event of an accidental overwrite, there's a 90-day recovery window before prior workflow artifacts are deleted.

Hi @cevich! 

On the Windows side, I am a little worried about the possibility of ending up with multiple builds of the same version in the wild. The way the Windows installer architecture works is that every bulid gets a UUID, and this is how Windows recognizes the package is already installed (displaying options like uninstalling, reinstalling, and so on). Additionally, there is a contract where each version is supposed to map to one UUID, so it gets very confused when the same version is presented. So for example, if you were attempt to install a different build of the same version, the user would get a strange failure (likely version policy violation), and then have to know to uninstall it before installing the new build. 

Another issue is it might trigger suspicion with various malware tools, when they see checksum variations for the same version. 

While there could be circumstances where we want/need to do this (e.g.  broken build), ideally we would prevent accidental or CI dups causing it, limiting it to just an intentional action (e.g. explicit setting of the overwrite flag idea you list)

Thanks!

Thanks so much Jason for the followup, I woudln't/couldn't have guessed any of that.  So the "existing installer" check is quite important then.  I wonder if the OSX environment is subject to the same/similar gotchas.  Since we don't (currently) have a check for that in place.  

@ashley-cui do you know?
As far as I'm aware, I don't think there's such a gotcha for the OSX installer, but I'm not an expert on this. I can double check.
